1. Intertrochanteric fracture left hip
Discharge Diagnoses:
Active Hospital Problems
 *Closed left hip fracture, initial encounter (HCC)
Hospital Course: 49 y.o. female with a past history significant for iron-deficiency anemia/gastric sleeve
surgery, migraine admitted on 2/25/2023 with pain left hip after tripping and falling over speeding bump of the
parking lot At Wallgreen , immediately she felt pain on the left hip with unable to move or walk brought to the
emergency room via her daughter car,
Emergency room course
Vitals T 98.4°, HR 118, RR 11, BP 147/67
Lab sodium 135 hemoglobin 7.2 hematocrit 23.8
X-ray left hip showed intertrochanteric fracture of the left femoral neck
CT pelvis shows
Acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.
Treatment given morphine
On also ketamine muscle relaxant
On February 26, 2023
Underwent open reduction and internal fixation of intertrochanteric fracture.

Discharge Summary by Ibrahim, Kamal, MD at 02/28/23 0913 (continued)
VIEWS: Two views left hip. COMPARISON: No comparison. HISTORY: Postop left hip.
FINDINGS/IMPRESSION: Hardware present within the left hip related to open reduction and internal fixation of
intertrochanteric fracture. Hardware is intact and in expected position. Essure device projected over the
pelvis. Visualized bony pelvic rings are intact.
CT PELVIS WO CONTRAST
Result Date: 2/25/2023
INDICATION: FX, needs further eval COMPARISON: Radiographs same day TECHNIQUE: Volumetric CT
scan of the abdomen, and pelvis without intravenous contrast. 2D axial, sagittal, and coronal reformats were
performed. FINDINGS: Bladder: Unremarkable. Solid pelvic organs: Unremarkable. Visualized Bowel: No
dilation or wall thickening. No extraluminal fluid or gas. Lymph nodes: None enlarged by CT size criteria.
Vessels: Unremarkable. Bones: There is an acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.
Adjacent small hematoma and soft tissue swelling noted.
IMPRESSION: Acute comminuted intratrochanteric fracture of the left femur.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with •Fall •Hip Pain
HPI
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female who presents for evaluation of left hip pain. She was leaving work,
tripped on curb and fell. Denies head injury. No headache or neck pain. States has pain exclusively to L hip
and denies other injuries. No anticoagulant use.

CONSTITUTIONAL: No complaints of fever, chills,or weakness
EYES: No complaints of discharge
ENT: No complaints of sore throat or ear pain

MUSCULOSKELETAL: No complaints of back pain
SKIN:Denies rash or lesions.
NEUROLOGIC: No complaints of headache, focal weakness, or sensory changes
ENDOCRINE: No complaints of polyuria or polydipsia
LYMPHATIC: No complaints of swollen glands
GENITOURINARY: No complaints of urinary frequency or hematuria

Psychiatric: Affect normal, Judgment normal, Mood normal.
Ketamine was given for pain control while we repositioned the LLE for comfort. She was having severe
cramping in the L thigh and hip and buttock due to the fracture and the positioning of the leg which she would
not allow us to manipulate prior to sedation. Adequate sedation achieved, Leg repositioned and patient much
more comfortable.

Medical Decision Making
Ms. Brasher presented after falling today. She reported a mechanical fall, no syncope. She had left hip pain,
we did discover a left hip fracture. I discussed with orthopedics provider on-call and she will be admitted, plan
for surgery tomorrow at 8:00 a.m..
Closed fracture of left hip, initial encounter (HCC): acute illness or injury
Amount and/or Complexity of Data Reviewed
External Data Reviewed: labs.
Labs: ordered.
 Details: CBC demonstrated anemia, hemoglobin was 7. Patient reported history of anemia that required
blood transfusions due to iron deficiency in the past. She felt that this was closed her baseline. I examined her
again myself after this result and did not note any signs of internal bleeding. She was also examined by an
orthopedist in internist and we will do suspect that this is simply iron deficiency. I ordered 1 unit of blood prior
to surgery.
Radiology: ordered.
 Details: Ct was ordered to further evaluate the fracture demonstrated on Xray.
Risk
Prescription drug management.

orthopaedic injury while being treated by hospitalist service.

Plan
Risks and benefits were explained to these included but not limited to bleeding, infection, damage to the
surrounding structures, need for further procedures, anesthetic effect or events, reflex sympathetic dystrophy,
DVT, PE, pneumonia, pain, decreased range of motion, decreased functional strength, functional impairment,
incapacitation, disability, malunion, nonunion, hardware failure, hardware breakage, hardware migration,
fracture, re-fracture, loosening of hardware, need for revision surgeries, dissatisfaction, unforeseen problems in
the future, unforeseen complications in the future, arthrosis, arthritis, fibrosis, stiffness, nerve damage, blood
vessel damage, hematoma, seroma, wound problems, wound dehiscence, numbness, tingling, capsular,
ligamentous, cartilage, bony tendinous, muscular damage, limb inequality, cosmetic deformities. At no time
were guarantees stated or implied. Plan for operative fixation of fracture tomorrow morning with Dr. Lowery
NPO at MN
Consent orders placed
OR staff notified

Diagnosis and treatment explained in detail and questions answered.

DATE OF PROCEDURE:
[DOB-174]
SURGEON:
Douglas Lowery, MD
HPI:
A 49-year-old female who sustained a closed left intertrochanteric hip
fracture. Risks, benefits and alternatives explained to the patient. Marked
preoperatively for IM nailing of hip fracture. See the H and P for details.
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:
Left closed intertrochanteric hip fracture.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:
Left closed intertrochanteric hip fracture.
PROCEDURE:
Left intramedullary nailing of hip fracture laterally.
None.
BLOOD LOSS:
Minimal.
SPECIMENS:
None.
FINDINGS:
Closed intertrochanteric hip fracture.
COMPONENTS USED:
Synthes short trochanteric femoral nail, 9 mm x 135 degrees, with an 85 mm
head and neck device, and a 34 mm distal locking screw.
MEDICATIONS:
Preoperative antibiotics.
DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE:
The patient was brought to the operative room, prepped and draped in normal
sterile routine orthopedic fashion following anesthesia per the anesthesia
department. The patient was placed on the fracture table, and appropriate
Thu Sep 21, 2023 12:12 PM

DEACONESS HENDERSON
HOSPITAL
1305 N ELM ST
HENDERSON KY 42420-
2783
IP Encounter Report
Brasher, Kimberly K
MRN: 469590, DOB: 3/25/1973, Sex: F
Adm: 2/25/2023, D/C: 2/28/2023
Op Note - Operative and Procedure Notes (continued)
Op Note signed by Lowery, Douglas, MD at 03/03/23 0836 (continued)
reduction maneuvers were performed to align the hip fracture. A small
incision was made, based on the greater trochanter just proximal and
posterior. A sharp dissection was taken down to the gluteal fascia, which was
 then split in line with its fibers. The greater trochanter was identified
with direct palpation. FluoroScan was utilized to facilitate placement of a
guidewire in the greater trochanter at the appropriate place and taken from
the greater trochanter into the femoral shaft. This was checked on AP and
lateral radiographs and found to be appropriate. Next, the opening reamer was
 used to open up the insertion site and to make sure there was an appropriate
amount of room for the nail to enter the shaft. After appropriate
preparation, the nail listed in findings section was placed in the canal with
attention to depth of insertion. This was checked on AP and lateral
radiographs. Through a second small stab incision on the lateral aspect of
the flare of the greater trochanter, we bring our guidewire from the flare of
the greater trochanter up through the femoral neck and into the femoral head
on AP and lateral radiographs. Once an appropriate measurement was obtained,
drill and insertion of the head and neck component was placed. Once this was
appropriate and in place, we tightened the locking screw. Once both of these
were checked on AP and lateral radiographs and found to be in appropriate
position, we then used the guide on static and, with a small stab incision,
made our interlocking screw distally. We checked this on AP and lateral
radiographs as well. Copious irrigation with normal saline then followed. We
 removed the insertion hardware. We made sure that our last set of AP and
lateral radiographs showed good hardware placement. We then copiously
irrigated with normal saline once again, closed the fascia with 2-0 Vicryl in
interrupted deep buried fashion and closed the skin. The dressings included
coverlets, and the patient was then taken to the recovery room. ______________________________ Douglas Lowery, MD

Physical Exam
Inspection: Skin warm to touch. Normal color and turgor. Skin is intact/incision appears to be healing.
Wound: Dressings in place, dry with minimal drainage. No signs of infection.
Palpation: No calf tenderness or signs of DVT.
ROM: Displays AROM dorsiflexion upper and lower extremities bilaterally.
Neurovascular: Distal light sensation is intact. Distal pulses intact. No signs of compartment syndrome noted.
ASSESSMENT: Status Post: Left hip intramedullary nail
PLAN:
Labs, imaging, and medications reviewed today. Hemoglobin 7.9 this morning. Appears much less sedated
today compared to yesterday. States that she is ready to leave hospital. Is declining facility level placement
for rehab needs. States she plans to go to outpatient therapy and family member will be taking care of her
upon discharge home. We had briefly discussed yesterday that due to her chronic anemia we may discharge
her home on full strength aspirin but since her hemoglobin is improving I feel that it is better to place her on
Xarelto for the next 30 days for DVT prophylaxis. Explained to patient and family members this will provide her
with better protection against blood clots. They were both in agreement. PT/OT therapy order placed and
printed for patient to take to preferred outpatient therapy facility. Electronic Rx of Xarelto sent to pharmacy on
file. Patient to follow up in our office in 2 weeks for recheck with x-rays and staple removal. To call the office
with any concerns. Being discharged home today. Discussed treatment plan with patient/family. Answered all
questions to the satisfaction of patient/family. Nursing/rehab staff to contact with any questions and/or
concerns.
Weight Bearing Status: Weight-bearing as tolerated left lower extremity.
ROM: AROM as tolerated to left lower extremity.
Splint/Brace: Rolling walker
DVT Prophylaxis: Lovenox to transition to Xarelto on discharge

Progress Notes - Progress Notes (continued)
Progress Notes by Gish, James B, NP at 02/27/23 1154 (continued)
CBC drawn this morning just resulted. Hemoglobin 7.8, this is up from 7.2 on 02/25 but patient did receive a
blood transfusion prior to surgery. Would recommend repeating labs in morning to evaluate if another
transfusion may be needed. PT visit this morning was essentially postponed due to patient not tolerating any
movement due to pain.
Encouraged patient to use oral pain medications ordered instead of IV Dilaudid as this seems to be causing
more sedation than as needed. Would like to continue the subcutaneous Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis while in
hospital. We may switch to full-strength aspirin twice daily on discharge for continued prophylaxis as there is a
chronic anemia concern.
Will continue to follow.
Weight Bearing Status: Weight-bearing as tolerated left lower extremity.
ROM: AROM as tolerated to left lower extremity.
Splint/Brace: Rolling walker
DVT Prophylaxis: Lovenox.
Follow-up Appointment: 2-3 weeks post-op in the office.
Discharge: pending, see plan.
Brad Gish, NP
Orthopaedic Associates

Narrative
Pt was admitted with left hip fx following a fall. She received ORIF. Pt requires encouragement for any activity
with nurse present to provide pain medication. She voices want to try things on her own and does not tolerate
touching of left LE. After extensive time with attempt EOB, pt refuses further assistance with transfer, even with
reports of unable to continue. Finally asking to be left alone. Pt left in bed with friend present and PCT plan to
check back with her. Will continue to follow.
Recommendations
Therapy Recommendations: Pt would benefit from continued therapy at a facility level

Basic Mobility T-Scale Score: 16.59 (02/27/23 0900)

Patient/Family education, Gait training, ROM, Bed mobility, Neuro re-education, Home exercise program, Body
mechanics, Safety education
PT Frequency: 5x/week, 7x/week

Home Layout: One level, Stairs to enter with rails

OCCUPATIONAL THERAPY
ASSESSMENT NOTE
Narrative
Pt admitted after fall resulting in L hip fx. She underwent ORIF on L hip 2/26/23. She is WBAT to LLE,
RN is providing IV pain meds upon arrival. Pt requires increased time to participate with therapy due to wanting
to don underwear and pants and being very particular about mobility and using her L leg. She insists on doing
as much as possible for herself before requiring assist from PT/OT. She is able to scoot self to side of bed
supervision/touch assist with increased time. Attempted to assist pt with sitting EOB with increased time and
assist x3 people. Pt becomes very anxious, pushing back, and screaming in pain. Did not attempt further
mobility due to safety. Will attempt further mobility at a later time as able.
Recommendations
Recommendation: Pt would benefit from continued therapy at a facility level
Outcome Measure
Daily Activity Raw Score : 15
Daily Activity T-Scale Score: 36.69
Assessment
Assessment: Decreased ADL status, Decreased UE strength, Decreased safe judgement during ADL,
Decreased activity tolerance, Decreased self-care trans, Decreased high-level ADLs
Plan
OT Frequency: 3x/week, 5x/week
Treatment Interventions: ADL retraining, Functional transfer training, UE strengthening/ROM, Activity
tolerance, Patient/Family training, Equipment eval/education, Fine motor coordination activities, Compensatory
technique education, Energy conservations/work simplification, HEP, IADLs, Safety education
Prior Level of Function
Self Care (New): Independent
Indoor Mobility (Ambulation) (New): Independent
Stairs (New): Independent
Functional Cognition (New): Independent

NARRATIVE:
49 year old female who presented with a Fx Hip.
ASSESSMENT:
Met with patient's friend at bedside. She reports the plan moving forward is for the patient to go back home with
her in Bowling Green , Ky where she will see to patient's care and get her in outpatient PT. Her friend states
she has a walker , BSC , and raised toilet seat for use at her home for the patient as she recently had a hip
replacement in the past. Spoke with B. Gish APRN/ Ortho and he is aware of this plan and agreeable. Will
follow.
PLAN:
Home with friend

position. Essure device projected over the pelvis. Visualized bony pelvic
rings are intact.

Reason for hospitalization (as dictated at the time of admission)
HPI altered mental status
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female with PMH of closed left intertrochanteric fracture s/p ORIF [DOB-109],
chronic iron-deficiency anemia, gastric bypass surgery, migraine headaches who was brought to the ED after
being found unresponsive by her daughter at home. Daughter reports that patient received Norco 5.0 mg x 20
tabs on 3/7. She had a follow up with her orthopedic surgeon yesterday (3/9) and received an additional 28
tablets of Norco 7.5 mg. Daughter believes that patient took all of these since getting her prescription filled.
 Per ED documentation patient was awake and alert with GCS 15 on arrival but slow to respond with slurred
Thu Sep 21, 2023 12:12 PM
Admit -- Condition-- ED Disposition Comment

Discharge Summary - Discharge Summary (continued)
Discharge Summary by Vasser-Smith, Montubua, MD at 03/12/23 1137 (continued)
speech. She did not require Narcan.
Vitals on arrival notable for pulse 155. Notable labs: Hemoglobin 7.5, INR 2.0, AST 48, TSH 5.80 with normal
free T4, acetaminophen level 41. CT head and CT cervical spine negative for acute process. X-ray of the left
hip showed intact hardware. Chest x-ray was negative. ED physician discussed case with poison control and
patient was started on NAC protocol in addition to Protonix 40 mg and 1 L normal saline bolus. Hospitalist
service was contacted for admission.
Hospital Course:
Kimberly K BrasherN was admitted to the ICU in guarded condition. She was started on NAC
protocol. Her AST returned to normal levels and her acetaminophen level was undetectable by the next
morning. Her INR fell from 2.0 down to 1.4, hemoglobin remains stable at her baseline of
approximately 7-7.5. I do recommend she follow-up with Hematology with her history of iron deficient
anemia. Patient had completed her NAC, and given all numbers returned to normal range, it was
discontinued
Patient adamantly denies any form of self-harm, appears to be quite embarrassed for the events that
happened. She is unsure how she became so confused and overdosed on the medication. She is
eager to be able to get back home, understands she will not have opiates to help control her pain, and
this does bring her some anxiety. Chart review shows previous visits to Psychiatry where notes do not
reveal any prior SI/HI. Recommendation is to follow-up outpatient psychiatry. Patient was offered
Ultram at discharge but she declined.
PT did recommend inpatient rehab however the patient is adamant that she does not want facility level
rehab and will follow-up as outpatient with her previous order for PT.
Hypokalemia was noted on the morning of 3/11. Potassium 2.8. Patient was given oral and IV
replacement. On the day of discharge potassium has normalized at 3.8.
Physical Exam:
GENERAL: Frail appearing white female in no acute distress.
CHEST: Clear to auscultation bilaterally without wheezes, stridor, crackles. Adequate inspiratory effort and air
flow. No accessory muscle use with breathing.
CARDIAC: Regular S1-S2. No murmur, rub, or gallop. No JVD, cyanosis, or leg edema.
ABDOMEN: Bowel sounds are audible. Soft, nontender, nondistended. No hepatosplenomegaly.
MUSCULOSKELETAL: S/p left hip ORIF, incision c/d/i
SKIN: Warm and dry. No rashes.
NEUROLOGIC: Awake and alert speech is clear quite conversant. 5/5 strength in bilateral upper and lower
extremities. Cranial nerves 2-12 are intact. No gross sensory deficits.
PSYCHIATRIC: Alert and oriented x3. Pleasant mood and affect.
Condition upon Discharge: Improved
Disposition: Home with outpatient PT
Instructions for follow up care:
Thu Sep 21, 2023 12:12 PM

FINDINGS:
There is mild wedging of the C4 vertebral body anteriorly. This is age
indeterminate. Sagittal and coronal alignment are otherwise intact. The
remainder of the vertebral body heights are intact. Intervertebral disc spaces
are preserved.
Impression:
IMPRESSION:
Mild anterior wedging of the C4 vertebral body, which is age indeterminate.
This is favored to be chronic. However, if there is further clinical concern
for trauma, MRI can be obtained to assess for the presence of edema.

Author: Jones, William T, MD Service: Emergency Author Type: Physician
Filed: 03/10/23 1205 Date of Service: 03/10/23 0759 Status: Signed
Editor: Jones, William T, MD (Physician) .CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with •Drug Overdose
HPI
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female who presents with last known well of 8:00 p.m. last night. She had left
hip surgery 2 weeks ago. She was prescribed new prescription of Norco yesterday. Her daughter filled it. At
2:30 a.m. this morning daughter noticed that she was confused thinking that she was working in nursing home.
She then fell in her bedroom. Patient is confused and altered. Disoriented answers appropriate age and
name. Confabulates story though from patient. She denies pain anywhere other than her left hip. Patient
denies any suicide ideation or self-harm ideation. She states she is not take anything to harm herself. Patient
states that she thinks this is due to the medication. Daughter notes that her recent Norco bottles are
completely emptying they were filled yesterday. She is also prescribed Flexeril and Xarelto to prevent blood
clots. Denies alcohol or illicit drugs. Daughter states no concern for self-harm.
Estimate she took 28 tablets of norco 7.5-325 between 8pm and 2:30am: That is 9100mg tylenol, 150mg/kg =
8550mg is toxic dose. Poison control notified
REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Twelve point review of systems obtained and otherwise unremarkable except as above.

The initial differential diagnosis for this patient's presentation include: Intracranial hemorrhage, polypharmacy,
accidental overdose
Each of the above mentioned unique tests were ordered by myself for workup of this patient.
I have reviewed the external records available to me, pertaining to this patient's care.
I have reviewed the results of each unique tests mentioned above.
The Independent Historian, daughter, was used to gather history pertaining to this patient's care.
My Independent Interpretation of this patient's history, exam and workup (including laboratory, radiology or
ancillary testing) are most consistent with medication overdose accidental along with concern for toxic
ingestion of Tylenol in her Norco. Resulting altered mental status. No intracranial hemorrhage. Unknown time
of ingestion thus will treat empirically with N-acetylcysteine IV protocol. No blood in the stool. Anemia for last

CHIEF COMPLAINT/ REASON FOR ADMISSION
Altered mental status
ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
Problem List:
Active Hospital Problems
 Medication overdose, accidental or unintentional, initial encounter
Accidental overdose of opioids and acetaminophen
Acetaminophen poisoning
Metabolic encephalopathy · Continue NAC protocol, repeat CMP, INR, acetaminophen level in the morning. · Monitor in ICU. No indication for Narcan at this time. · Monitor on telemetry
Left intertrochanteric fracture · S/p ORIF 2/26/23 · Continue Xarelto for DVT prophylaxis · Hold Tylenol and narcotic pain medication · Daughter is concerned patient may need rehab; PT/OT/case management consults placed
Iron deficiency anemia · Patient taking ferrous fumarate · Hgb at baseline, follow with daily labs
DVT Prophylaxis: Xarelto
Code Status: Full Code
Medication Reconciliation Completed: yes was based on patient and family report, chart review
Time: 56 minutes

H&P - History & Physical (continued)
H&P by Walker, William M, DO at 03/10/23 1032 (continued)
Impression and plan was discussed with patient, nursing and daughter . All questions were answered.
HISTORY
History obtained from: chart review and the patient
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female with PMH of closed left intertrochanteric fracture s/p ORIF [DOB-116],
chronic iron-deficiency anemia, gastric bypass surgery, migraine headaches who was brought to the ED after
being found unresponsive by her daughter at home. Daughter reports that patient received Norco 5.0 mg x 20
tabs on 3/7. She had a follow up with her orthopedic surgeon yesterday (3/9) and received an additional 28
tablets of Norco 7.5 mg. Daughter believes that patient took all of these since getting her prescription filled.
Per ED documentation patient was awake and alert with GCS 15 on arrival but slow to respond with slurred
speech. She did not require Narcan.
Vitals on arrival notable for pulse 155. Notable labs: Hemoglobin 7.5, INR 2.0, AST 48, TSH 5.80 with normal
free T4, acetaminophen level 41. CT head and CT cervical spine negative for acute process. X-ray of the left
hip showed intact hardware. Chest x-ray was negative. ED physician discussed case with poison control and
patient was started on NAC protocol in addition to Protonix 40 mg and 1 L normal saline bolus. Hospitalist
service was contacted for admission.
Review of Systems: A complete 10 system review of systems was completed and negative except as noted in
the HPI or here.
Concurrent Medical/ Past Medical History
Past Medical History:
Diagnosis Date •Anemia •HGSIL on cytologic smear of cervix •Migraine
Past Surgical History
 has a past surgical history that includes gastric bypass surgery (2008); breast enhancement surgery (2012);
cholecystectomy (2005); foot surgery; and femur fracture surgery (Left, 2/26/2023).
Home Medications
No outpatient medications have been marked as taking for the 3/10/23 encounter
(Hospital Encounter).
Allergies
Allergies
Allergen Reactions •Penicillins

BestPractice Advisories Brasher, Kimberly K [469590]
Progress Notes - Progress Notes
Progress Notes by Nesmith, Talia J, MD at 03/11/23 0804
Author: Nesmith, Talia J, MD Service: Physician to Physician Author Type: Physician
Filed: 03/11/23 1413 Date of Service: 03/11/23 0804 Status: Signed
Editor: Nesmith, Talia J, MD (Physician) . Deaconess Progress Note
Patient Name: Kimberly K Brasher
MRN: 469590
DOB: 3/25/1973
Date of Admission: 3/10/2023
Date of Service: 3/11/2023
PMD: No primary care provider on file.
Reason for hospitalization
Kimberly K Brasher is a 49 y.o. female with PMH of closed left intertrochanteric fracture s/p ORIF [DOB-178],
chronic iron-deficiency anemia, gastric bypass surgery, migraine headaches who was brought to the ED after
being found unresponsive by her daughter at home. Daughter reports that patient received Norco 5.0 mg x 20
tabs on 3/7. She had a follow up with her orthopedic surgeon yesterday (3/9) and received an additional 28
tablets of Norco 7.5 mg. Daughter believes that patient took all of these since getting her prescription filled.
 Per ED documentation patient was awake and alert with GCS 15 on arrival but slow to respond with slurred
speech. She did not require Narcan.
Vitals on arrival notable for pulse 155. Notable labs: Hemoglobin 7.5, INR 2.0, AST 48, TSH 5.80 with normal
free T4, acetaminophen level 41. CT head and CT cervical spine negative for acute process. X-ray of the left
hip showed intact hardware. Chest x-ray was negative. ED physician discussed case with poison control and
patient was started on NAC protocol in addition to Protonix 40 mg and 1 L normal saline bolus. Hospitalist
service was contacted for admission.
Hospital course
Patient was admitted to the ICU in guarded condition. She was started on NAC protocol. Her AST
returned to normal levels and her acetaminophen level was undetectable by the next morning. Her INR
fell from 2.0 down to 1.4, hemoglobin remains stable at her baseline of approximately 7-7.5. I do
recommend she follow-up with Hematology with her history of iron deficient anemia. Patient had
completed her NAC, and given all numbers returned to normal range, it was discontinued
Patient adamantly denies any form of self-harm, appears to be quite embarrassed for the events that
happened. She is unsure how she became so confused and overdosed on the medication. She is
eager to be able to get back home, understands she will not have opiates to help control her pain, and
this does bring her some anxiety. Chart review shows previous visits to Psychiatry where notes do not
reveal any prior SI/HI. Recommendation is to follow-up outpatient psychiatry.
Hypokalemia was noted on the morning of 3/11. Potassium 2.8. Patient was given oral and IV
Thu Sep 21, 2023 12:12 PM

PHYSICAL THERAPY
Patient: Kimberly K Brasher
Age: 49 y.o.
DOB: 3/25/1973
Narrative
Patient admitted after an accidental overdose of pain meds. Patient fractured her left hip 2 weeks ago and
underwent ORIF 2/2/6/23.
Patient lives with her adult child and currently walking short distances with a rolling walker. Prior to hip fx
patient was independent.
Patient's activity is limited by pain and weakness. Motivated to get up and walk to commode with assist and
walker.
Recommendations
Therapy Recommendations: Pt would benefit from continued therapy at a facility level
Outcome Measure
Basic Mobility Raw Score: 17 (03/11/23 1057)
Basic Mobility T-Scale Score: 39.67 (03/11/23 1057)
Plan
Treatment/Interventions: Transfer training, Gait training, Bed mobility
PT Frequency: 5x/week, 7x/week
Prior Level of Function
Permanent Residence: Home

Answer Hypoglycemia Treatment Oral (15:15 Rule)
Oral Treatment Options 15 Grams FAST Carbohydrates
Snack (if meal greater than 1 hour away)
Fast Carbohydrates 240 ml skim milk
120 ml unsweetened applejuice (chose for Renal Patients or elevated
K+)
120 ml regular soda
Snack for meal greater than 1 hour away 1 slice bread / toast
6 saltines (avoid in renal or low sodium diets)
3 squares of graham crackers
For Non-Renal Patient: 1 Tablespoon peanut butter (protein) may be
added to any of the above

Procalcitonin 0.07 <0.10 NG/ML — DHND
Comment: ====================
LOWER RESPIRATORY TRACT INFECTION
Antibiotics are strongly discouraged. Recommend repeat PCT if antibiotics not
 given and not improved in 6-12 hours. ==================== SEPSIS
Antibiotics are strongly discouraged.
Antibiotic INITIATION should not be withheld if highly suspicious for sepsis.
Recommend repeat PCT if antibiotics not given and not improved in 6-12 hours. ==================== Procalcitonin Normals -- — — DHND
Result:
GENERAL COMMENTS Antibiotic initiation should not be withheld for patients with sepsis or
pneumonia with radiographic evidence of infiltrates. A low procalcitonin should prompt the
investigation of non-bacterial or non-infectious causes of patient clinical status. Repeat low
 or declining procalcitonin (>80% from peak) may indicate an opportunity for antibiotic
de-escalation or discontinuation. Low procalcitonin levels in the presence of acute bronchitis,
 COPD exacerbations, and other related conditions generally indicates the absence of clinically
 significant bacterial infections. Please refer to the procalcitonin guidance document on DWeb
 under Quick Links - Antimicrobial Stewardship for additional information.
Reference Range Flag Lab
Salicylate Level <1.0 <20 MG/DL — DHND

Radiology Results
XR PELVIS W LATERAL HIP LEFT [314411487] Resulted: 03/10/23 1658, Result status: Final result
Ordering provider: Jones, William T, MD 03/10/23 0820 Resulted by: Morozov, Pavel, MD
Performed: 03/10/23 0858 - 03/10/23 0911 Accession number: DHHND5987878
Resulting lab: RAD/CARD
Narrative:
Indication:
Fall.
Recent hardware within the left neck.
COMPARISON: [DOB-185].
FINDINGS: There is left hip hardware, which appears to be grossly intact. The
intertrochanteric fracture is still visualized. There is moderate comminution.
When comparing with CT from [DOB-186], the examination is likely unchanged.
There does not appear to be any new fracture or dislocation.
Impression:
IMPRESSION:
Intact hardware following intramedullary nail placement within the left femur.
Overall, comminuted intertrochanteric fracture appears to be similar.
CT CERVICAL SPINE WO CONTRAST [314411491] Resulted: 03/10/23 1655, Result status: Final result
Ordering provider: Jones, William T, MD 03/10/23 0820 Resulted by: Morozov, Pavel, MD
Performed: 03/10/23 0849 - 03/10/23 0853 Accession number: DHHND5987868
Resulting lab: RAD/CARD
Narrative:
Indication:
altered mental status.
Fall on Xarelto.
COMPARISON: None.
TECHNIQUE: Axial images from the base of the skull through the thoracic inlet were
performed followed by 2D multiplanar reformats.
FINDINGS: There is mild wedging of the C4 vertebral body anteriorly. This is age
indeterminate. Sagittal and coronal alignment are otherwise intact. The
remainder of the vertebral body heights are intact. Intervertebral disc spaces
are preserved.
Impression:
IMPRESSION: Mild anterior wedging of the C4 vertebral body, which is age indeterminate.
This is favored to be chronic. However, if there is further clinical concern
for trauma, MRI can be obtained to assess for the presence of edema.
CT HEAD WO CONTRAST [314411490] Resulted: 03/10/23 1008, Result status: Final result

Resulting lab: RAD/CARD

Indication:
Fall.
On blood thinners.
Altered mental status.

followed by 2D multiplanar reformats.

Admin Instructions: Use the following steps to administer via IV push:
1. Reconstitute powder with 10 ml of saline, the resulting concentration
is 4 mg/ml.
2. Withdraw dose into syringe and administer over at least 2 minutes.
3. If you must administer through line with incompatible drips: - flush line with 5 ml of saline through lowest port
- administer through lowest port over 2 minutes - flush line with 5 ml of saline

SYMPTOMS OF WITHDRAWAL

STATES SHE IS ITCHING AND

EVANSVILLE IN 47710
Final
CT HIP LEFT WO CONTRAST 
Exam Date & Time: [DOB-201] 1100
INDICATION:
Left hip pain after IM nail. 
COMPARISON:
X-ray of the pelvis from 3/10/2023 
TECHNIQUE:
Axial images through the left hip were performed followed by 2D multiplanar 
reformats.
FINDINGS:
There is a left IM rod with a dynamic screw. Hardware appears to be intact. 
There is redemonstrated intertrochanteric fracture with displacement. Some 
callous formation is noted but fracture lucencies are still visualized.
Alignment appears to be intact as does the hardware. The left hip itself
appears to be intact as well. 
IMPRESSIONS:
1. Redemonstrated comminuted left intratrochanteric fracture following ORIF. 
Hardware is intact. Some healing is noted with fracture lucency still present. 
2. The left hip itself is unremarkable. No specific finding is noted to
account for patient's symptoms.
